Predictors of Scene Timing Performance Following Concussion

Multiple Regression Analysis — PSYC 6132

2026-03-31

a) Research Problem & Theoretical Rationale

Concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury that disrupts cognitive and sensorimotor processing. Individuals recovering from concussion commonly experience dizziness, impaired balance, slowed reaction times, and difficulties with visual-spatial navigation — all of which may impair performance on timed, real-world tasks.

The scene timing composite (8TimingComp) captures the efficiency with which a participant completes a structured scene task, providing an ecologically valid index of functional performance. We hypothesise that post-concussion symptom severity — as measured by validated clinical scales — will predict slower (i.e., worse) performance on this task, over and above the effects of demographic characteristics and time since injury.

b) Variables

Criterion variable

Variable Description Scale
8TimingComp Scene completion time composite Continuous (z-scored composite; higher = slower performance)

Predictors

Variable Description Scale
AGE Participant age Continuous (years)
Sex_Recode_F1M0 Biological sex Binary (0 = Male, 1 = Female)
DHI Dizziness Handicap Inventory Continuous (0–100; higher = greater dizziness handicap)
RPQ_3 Rivermead Post-Concussion Symptoms — 3 early symptoms Continuous (0–12; higher = more severe)
RPQ_13 Rivermead Post-Concussion Symptoms — 13 later symptoms Continuous (0–52; higher = more severe)
DaysSinceLastConcussion Days elapsed since most recent concussion (control variable) Continuous (days)

c) Regression Equation

The model takes the form:

\[\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1(\text{AGE}) + b_2(\text{Sex}) + b_3(\text{DHI}) + b_4(\text{RPQ\_3}) + b_5(\text{RPQ\_13}) + b_6(\text{DaysSinceLastConcussion}) + \varepsilon\]

Where \(\hat{Y}\) is the predicted scene timing composite, \(b_0\) is the intercept, \(b_1\)–\(b_6\) are the unstandardised regression coefficients, and \(\varepsilon\) is the residual error term.

Predictor b SE t p
(Intercept) Intercept -4.122 1.318 -3.129 0.004
AGE AGE 0.074 0.023 3.287 0.002
Sex_Recode_F1M0 Sex (F=1, M=0) 0.697 0.485 1.436 0.160
DHI DHI 0.024 0.012 1.926 0.063
RPQ_3 RPQ_3 0.123 0.125 0.983 0.332
RPQ_13 RPQ_13 -0.034 0.026 -1.305 0.201
DaysSinceLastConcussion Days Since Last Concussion 0.000 0.000 -1.699 0.098

DAG: Variable Relationships

d) Critical Test

The critical elements are the symptom severity predictors: DHI, RPQ_3, and RPQ_13.

The central hypothesis is that greater post-concussion symptom burden predicts worse (slower) scene timing performance, even after controlling for age, sex, and days since last concussion. Specifically, we expect one or more of \(b_3\) (DHI), \(b_4\) (RPQ_3), and \(b_5\) (RPQ_13) to be positive and statistically significant (p < .05), indicating that higher symptom scores are associated with a higher (worse) timing composite.

The inclusion of DaysSinceLastConcussion as a covariate is important: without it, the symptom coefficients could be confounded by recovery duration — symptom scores naturally decrease over time, and performance may improve independently of symptom level. Controlling for days since injury isolates the concurrent relationship between symptom severity and performance.

e) Assumption Checks

1. Linearity

Residuals vs. Fitted (composite)

Look for random scatter around the horizontal zero line. A systematic curve or U-shape indicates non-linearity in the model’s linear combination of predictors.

Added-Variable Plots (per predictor)

Each panel shows the unique linear relationship between one predictor and the outcome after partialling out all others. A roughly linear trend in each panel is expected; a curve flags a problem for that specific predictor.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)

2. Normality

Q-Q Plot of Residuals

Points should follow the diagonal reference line closely. Systematic S-curves or pronounced heavy tails indicate departures from normality. In small samples this can inflate Type I error rates and distort confidence intervals; in large samples the central limit theorem provides some protection.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)
If violated: Consider a log or Box-Cox transformation of the outcome, or use bootstrap confidence intervals.

3. Homoscedasticity

Scale-Location Plot

The LOESS line should be roughly horizontal with consistent spread across the range of fitted values. A rising trend or funnel shape indicates heteroscedasticity, which produces biased standard errors and distorts p-values and confidence intervals.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)
If violated: Use heteroscedasticity-consistent (robust) standard errors via the sandwich package, or apply a variance-stabilising transformation to the outcome.

4. Independence

Residuals vs. Observation Order

No systematic trend, wave, or drift across the observation sequence is expected. A pattern suggests autocorrelation or a data-collection order effect, which violates the assumption that observations are independent.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)
If violated: Consider mixed-effects models if data are clustered, or investigate whether data were collected in temporal batches.

5. Multicollinearity

Predictor Correlation Heat Map

VIF Bar Chart

Correlations approaching ±0.80 between predictors are concerning. RPQ_3 and RPQ_13 are likely to be moderately correlated given they are subscales of the same instrument. VIF > 5 indicates moderate concern; VIF > 10 indicates serious multicollinearity that inflates standard errors and destabilises coefficient estimates.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)
If violated: Consider combining RPQ_3 and RPQ_13 into a single total RPQ score, or use ridge regression to stabilise estimates under multicollinearity.

6. Fixed Predictors

Predictor Distributions

Distributions should be plausible and free of implausible extreme values that would suggest data-entry errors or gross measurement anomalies. Extreme outliers in any continuous predictor can exert undue leverage on the regression solution.

Verdict: (Update after inspecting plots)
If violated: Investigate flagged observations individually. Winsorisation or case deletion with sensitivity analysis are options for confirmed outliers.

f) Conclusion & Interpretation

Overall model fit: The model accounted for R² = 0.475 of the variance in scene timing performance (adjusted R² = 0.383), F(6, 34) = 5.14, p = 0.001.

Coefficient table (significant predictors highlighted):

Predictor b SE t p
(Intercept) Intercept -4.122 1.318 -3.129 0.004
AGE AGE 0.074 0.023 3.287 0.002
Sex_Recode_F1M0 Sex (F=1, M=0) 0.697 0.485 1.436 0.160
DHI DHI 0.024 0.012 1.926 0.063
RPQ_3 RPQ_3 0.123 0.125 0.983 0.332
RPQ_13 RPQ_13 -0.034 0.026 -1.305 0.201
DaysSinceLastConcussion Days Since Last Concussion 0.000 0.000 -1.699 0.098

A positive b coefficient indicates that higher scores on that variable are associated with a slower (worse) scene timing composite, holding all other predictors constant. Rows highlighted in blue are statistically significant at p < .05.

Assumptions summary:

Assumption Method Verdict If Violated
Linearity Residuals vs Fitted + avPlots (Update after inspecting plots) Log/Box-Cox transform outcome
Normality Q-Q plot (Update after inspecting plots) Log/Box-Cox transform; bootstrap CIs
Homoscedasticity Scale-Location plot (Update after inspecting plots) Robust (sandwich) standard errors
Independence Index plot (Update after inspecting plots) Mixed-effects model; investigate batch effects
Multicollinearity Correlation heat map + VIF (Update after inspecting plots) Combine RPQ subscales; ridge regression
Fixed Predictors Histograms (Update after inspecting plots) Investigate outliers; winsorise or exclude with sensitivity analysis
